teachingPoint: Llamas are usually larger with longer faces and banana-shaped ears. Alpacas are smaller and often have fluffier faces.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Llamas are usually larger than alpacas.
Llamas usually have longer faces.
Llamas often have banana-shaped ears.
Alpacas are usually smaller.
Alpacas often have fluffier faces.

teachingPoint: Butterflies often have thin antennae with clubbed tips and may rest with wings upright. Moths often have feathery or plain antennae and may rest with wings spread flatter.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Butterflies usually have thin antennae with clubbed tips.
Moths often have feathery or plain antennae.
Butterflies often rest with wings upright.
Moths often rest with wings spread flatter.
Butterflies are usually active during the day.
Moths are often active at night.

teachingPoint: Chipmunks are usually smaller and often have stripes on the face and back. Squirrels are usually larger with bushier tails.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Chipmunks are usually smaller than squirrels.
Chipmunks often have stripes on their face and back.
Squirrels usually have larger bushy tails.
Squirrels often climb trees more than chipmunks.

teachingPoint: Turtles often live in or near water and may have flatter shells. Tortoises live mostly on land and often have dome-shaped shells and sturdy legs.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Turtles often spend time in water.
Tortoises live mostly on land.
Turtles may have flatter, smoother shells.
Tortoises often have higher dome-shaped shells.
Tortoises usually have sturdy elephant-like legs.

teachingPoint: Frogs usually have smoother skin and longer legs for jumping. Toads usually have bumpier skin and shorter legs.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Frogs usually have smooth, moist skin.
Toads usually have dry, bumpy skin.
Frogs often have longer legs.
Toads often have shorter legs and hop lower.

teachingPoint: Crocodiles often have narrower V-shaped snouts. Alligators often have wider U-shaped snouts.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Crocodiles often have V-shaped snouts.
Alligators often have U-shaped snouts.
Crocodile teeth may show when the mouth is closed.
Alligators are usually darker in color.

teachingPoint: Dolphins usually have longer beaks and curved dorsal fins. Porpoises often have rounder faces and more triangular dorsal fins.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Dolphins often have a longer beak-like snout.
Porpoises usually have rounder faces.
Dolphins often have curved dorsal fins.
Porpoises often have more triangular dorsal fins.

teachingPoint: Dolphins are usually gray and have a dorsal fin. Belugas are white and have rounded foreheads with no dorsal fin.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Belugas are usually white.
Belugas have rounded foreheads called melons.
Most dolphins are gray.
Many dolphins have a dorsal fin.
Belugas do not have a true dorsal fin.

teachingPoint: Sea lions have visible ear flaps and can rotate their front flippers to walk better on land. Seals usually have no visible ear flaps and move more by belly-scooting on land.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Sea lions have visible ear flaps.
Seals usually do not have visible ear flaps.
Sea lions can walk on land using their flippers.
Seals often scoot on their bellies on land.

teachingPoint: Hares are usually larger with longer ears and legs. Rabbits are usually smaller and often have shorter ears.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Hares are usually larger than rabbits.
Hares often have longer ears.
Hares often have longer legs.
Rabbits are usually smaller and rounder.

teachingPoint: Bees are often fuzzier and rounder. Wasps are usually smoother, slimmer, and have a narrow waist.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Bees are often fuzzy.
Wasps are usually smoother and shinier.
Wasps often have a narrow waist.
Bees are often rounder and hairier.

teachingPoint: Cheetahs are built for speed and have black tear lines on their faces. Leopards are stockier and have rosette-shaped spots.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Cheetahs have black tear lines on the face.
Cheetahs have solid black spots.
Leopards have rosette-shaped spots.
Leopards are usually stockier and stronger-looking.

teachingPoint: Jaguars are usually heavier and have larger rosettes, often with dots inside. Leopards are slimmer and have smaller rosettes.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Jaguars are usually stockier than leopards.
Jaguars often have larger rosettes with dots inside.
Leopards are usually slimmer.
Leopards often have smaller rosettes without center dots.

teachingPoint: Octopuses have rounded bodies and eight arms. Squid have longer torpedo-shaped bodies, eight arms, and two longer tentacles.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Octopuses have rounded bodies.
Squid have long torpedo-shaped bodies.
Octopuses have eight arms.
Squid have eight arms plus two longer tentacles.

teachingPoint: A fork has pointed tines for picking up food. A spoon has a rounded bowl for scooping food or liquid.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Forks have pointed tines.
Spoons have rounded bowls.
Forks are good for poking food.
Spoons are good for scooping.

teachingPoint: Trucks often have an open cargo bed or separate cargo area. Vans usually have an enclosed body for people or cargo.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Trucks often have cargo beds.
Vans usually have enclosed cargo space.
Trucks may have a separate cab and bed.
Vans usually look like one enclosed box.

teachingPoint: Buses carry many passengers on routes. RVs are vehicles made for travel and living, often with windows and home-like features.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Buses are made to carry many passengers.
RVs are made for travel and camping.
RVs may have living spaces inside.
Buses often have rows of passenger seats.

teachingPoint: Helicopters use spinning rotors and can hover. Airplanes use wings and usually need forward motion to fly.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Helicopters have large spinning rotors.
Helicopters can hover in one place.
Airplanes have fixed wings.
Airplanes usually need a runway or forward speed.

teachingPoint: Violins are smaller and often played with a bow under the chin. Guitars are larger and usually strummed or picked.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Violins are usually played with a bow.
Violins are held under the chin.
Guitars are larger.
Guitars are usually strummed or picked.

teachingPoint: A bridge goes over an obstacle such as water or a road. A tunnel goes through or under something.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Bridges go over things.
Tunnels go through or under things.
Bridges are usually open to the sky.
Tunnels are enclosed passages.

teachingPoint: Castles are large fortified buildings with features like towers or walls. Houses are homes for everyday living.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Castles often have towers or battlements.
Castles were built for defense.
Houses are everyday homes.
Houses are usually smaller than castles.

teachingPoint: Rivers are flowing bodies of water. Lakes are larger bodies of water that usually stay in one place.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Rivers flow from one place to another.
Lakes are bodies of water surrounded by land.
Rivers are usually long and narrow.
Lakes are often wider and stiller.

teachingPoint: Mountains are usually taller and steeper. Hills are usually lower and more rounded.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Mountains are usually taller than hills.
Mountains often have steeper sides.
Hills are usually lower.
Hills often look more rounded.

teachingPoint: Clouds are made of tiny water droplets or ice in the sky. Smoke comes from burning material and often rises from a source.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Clouds are usually high in the sky.
Smoke often comes from fire or burning.
Clouds are made from water droplets or ice.
Smoke may look gray or dark and rise from a source.

teachingPoint: Deserts are very dry places with little rain. Beaches are sandy or rocky areas along water.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Deserts are very dry.
Beaches are next to water.
Deserts may have cactus or dry land.
Beaches often have waves or shoreline.

teachingPoint: Rocks are pieces of mineral or stone. Shells are hard outer coverings made by animals such as clams or snails.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Rocks are stone or mineral.
Shells often come from sea animals.
Shells may be curved or spiral-shaped.
Rocks often have rough or solid stone texture.

teachingPoint: Flowers are usually smaller plants with blossoms. Trees are larger plants with trunks, branches, and leaves.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Flowers often have colorful blossoms.
Trees have woody trunks.
Trees are usually much taller.
Flowers usually have softer stems.

teachingPoint: Lowercase b has the tall line on the left and the round belly on the right. Lowercase d has the round belly on the left and the tall line on the right.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Lowercase b has the line first, then the belly.
Lowercase d has the belly first, then the line.
The round part of b points right.
The round part of d points left.

teachingPoint: Lowercase p has a down line on the left and a round part on the right. Lowercase q has a round part on the left and a down line on the right.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Lowercase p has the line on the left.
Lowercase q has the line on the right.
The round part of p is on the right.
The round part of q is on the left.

teachingPoint: Lowercase m has humps that go up from the baseline. Lowercase w has points that dip down like a double v.
distinguishingCharacteristics: m usually has rounded humps.
w usually has pointed dips.
m sits like two small arches.
w looks like two v shapes together.

teachingPoint: A 6 has its loop at the bottom. A 9 has its loop at the top.
distinguishingCharacteristics: 6 has the loop on the bottom.
9 has the loop on the top.
6 curls upward into the loop.
9 curls downward from the loop.

teachingPoint: A 2 usually curves at the top and slopes down to a base. A 5 usually has a top line and a lower curve.
distinguishingCharacteristics: 2 often starts with a curve.
5 often has a straight top.
2 slopes down toward the base.
5 has a lower rounded part.

teachingPoint: Lowercase l is a tall simple line. Uppercase I may have top and bottom bars depending on the font.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Lowercase l is often one simple vertical line.
Uppercase I may have top and bottom bars.
Uppercase I is a capital letter.
Lowercase l is a lowercase letter.

teachingPoint: A map is a flat picture of places. A globe is a round model of Earth.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Maps are flat.
Globes are round.
Maps can show many kinds of places.
Globes show Earth as a sphere.

teachingPoint: A city is a community or place where many people live. A country is a larger area with its own government and borders.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Cities are places within countries.
Countries are larger than cities.
Countries have borders and governments.
Cities often have many buildings and streets.

teachingPoint: Fruits often grow from flowers and contain seeds. Vegetables are other edible plant parts such as roots, stems, or leaves.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Fruits often contain seeds.
Fruits often grow from flowers.
Vegetables can be roots, stems, leaves, or other plant parts.
Vegetables are not always sweet.

teachingPoint: Mammals usually have hair or fur and feed milk to babies. Reptiles usually have scales and are cold-blooded.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Mammals usually have hair or fur.
Mammals feed milk to their babies.
Reptiles usually have scales.
Reptiles are cold-blooded.

teachingPoint: Herbivores eat plants. Carnivores eat other animals.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Herbivores eat plants.
Carnivores eat meat or other animals.
Herbivores often have flat teeth for grinding.
Carnivores often have sharper teeth for tearing.

teachingPoint: Combs usually have one row of teeth for separating hair. Brushes usually have many bristles spread over a wider head for smoothing hair.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Combs usually have one row of teeth.
Brushes usually have many bristles.
Combs are often flat and narrow.
Brushes often have a wider head and a handle.
Combs are good for parting or separating hair.
Brushes are good for smoothing hair.

teachingPoint: Flying squirrels are rodents with large dark eyes and a flatter, squirrel-like tail. Sugar gliders are marsupials with a dark stripe on the head and back and a long fluffy tail.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Flying squirrels are rodents, while sugar gliders are marsupials.
Sugar gliders often have a dark stripe running from the nose over the head and back.
Flying squirrels usually have a flatter, squirrel-like tail.
Sugar gliders often have a longer fluffy tail.
Both can glide using skin flaps, so look closely at the face markings and tail.

teachingPoint: Leopards are usually slimmer with smaller rosettes. Jaguars are usually stockier and often have larger rosettes with dots inside.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Leopards are usually slimmer than jaguars.
Leopards often have smaller rosette-shaped spots.
Jaguars are usually stockier and heavier-looking.
Jaguars often have larger rosettes with dots inside.
Jaguars often have broader, stronger-looking heads.

teachingPoint: Lungs help the body breathe air. The liver is a large organ in the upper belly that helps process nutrients and clean the blood.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Lungs are usually shown as two air-filled organs in the chest.
The liver is usually shown as one large organ on the right side of the upper belly.
Lungs connect to the windpipe.
The liver sits below the ribs and above the stomach.

teachingPoint: The liver is a large organ on the right side of the upper belly. The spleen is smaller and usually sits on the left side near the stomach.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The liver is larger than the spleen.
The liver is usually on the right side of the upper belly.
The spleen is usually on the left side.
The spleen is often shown as a smaller purple or red oval organ.

teachingPoint: Kidneys are bean-shaped organs that come as a pair. The liver is a large single organ in the upper belly.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Kidneys are bean-shaped.
Kidneys usually come in a left and right pair.
The liver is one large organ.
The liver is usually shown under the right ribs.

teachingPoint: The heart pumps blood and is usually shown in the middle of the chest. The lungs help with breathing and are usually shown as a left and right pair.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The heart is one muscular pump.
Lungs come as a pair.
The heart is usually between the lungs.
Lungs connect to the windpipe and airways.

teachingPoint: The stomach is a pouch that holds food after swallowing. The intestines are long looping tubes that continue digestion.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The stomach looks like a curved pouch.
The intestines look like long coiled tubes.
The stomach is higher in the belly.
The intestines fill much of the lower belly.

teachingPoint: The brain controls thinking and body signals. The heart pumps blood through the body.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The brain is inside the skull.
The heart is inside the chest.
The brain has folded-looking tissue.
The heart is often shown as a muscular pump.

teachingPoint: Ribs are curved bones around the chest. The spine is the column of bones running down the back.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Ribs are curved bones around the chest.
The spine is a vertical column down the back.
Ribs attach near the spine.
The spine is made of stacked vertebrae.

teachingPoint: The skull protects the brain in the head. The pelvis is the ring-like bone structure at the hips.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The skull is in the head.
The pelvis is at the hips.
The skull protects the brain.
The pelvis looks like a bony bowl or ring.

teachingPoint: The elbow bends the arm. The knee bends the leg.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The elbow is in the middle of the arm.
The knee is in the middle of the leg.
Elbows connect upper arm and forearm.
Knees connect thigh and lower leg.

teachingPoint: The wrist connects the hand to the arm. The ankle connects the foot to the leg.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The wrist is next to the hand.
The ankle is next to the foot.
Wrists help hands bend.
Ankles help feet bend.

teachingPoint: The shoulder connects the arm to the body. The hip connects the leg to the body.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Shoulders are near the top of the body.
Hips are lower on the body.
Shoulders connect arms.
Hips connect legs.

teachingPoint: Hands are used for grabbing and have fingers. Feet are used for standing and walking and have toes.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Hands have fingers.
Feet have toes.
Hands are at the ends of arms.
Feet are at the ends of legs.

teachingPoint: The thumb is the short side digit that can touch the other fingers. Fingers are the longer digits of the hand.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The thumb is shorter and set apart.
Fingers are usually longer and grouped together.
The thumb helps pinch and grip.
Most hands have one thumb and four fingers.

teachingPoint: Toes are on the feet. Fingers are on the hands.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Toes are on feet.
Fingers are on hands.
Toes are usually shorter.
Fingers help grab and point.

teachingPoint: The upper arm is between the shoulder and elbow. The forearm is between the elbow and wrist.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The upper arm is above the elbow.
The forearm is below the elbow.
The upper arm connects to the shoulder.
The forearm connects to the wrist.

teachingPoint: The thigh is the upper part of the leg. The shin is the front of the lower leg.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The thigh is above the knee.
The shin is below the knee.
The thigh is usually larger and more muscular.
The shin is the front of the lower leg.

teachingPoint: Eyes are used for seeing. Ears are used for hearing.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Eyes are on the front of the face.
Ears are on the sides of the head.
Eyes have pupils and irises.
Ears have outer folds for hearing.

teachingPoint: The nose is used for smelling and breathing. The mouth is used for eating, speaking, and breathing.
distinguishingCharacteristics: The nose is above the mouth.
The mouth has lips and teeth.
The nose has nostrils.
The mouth opens wider for eating and talking.

teachingPoint: Teeth are hard structures used for biting and chewing. The tongue is a soft muscle used for tasting, speaking, and moving food.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Teeth are hard and white.
The tongue is soft and muscular.
Teeth line the mouth.
The tongue sits inside the mouth.

teachingPoint: Cats are often smaller with retractable claws and pointed ears. Dogs vary widely but often have longer snouts and are known for barking.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Cats often have sharper pointed ears.
Dogs often have longer snouts.
Cats meow.
Dogs bark.

teachingPoint: Mice are usually smaller with more delicate bodies. Rats are usually larger with thicker tails and bodies.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Mice are usually smaller than rats.
Rats are usually larger and heavier.
Mice often have thinner tails.
Rats often have thicker tails and bigger heads.

teachingPoint: Horses are usually larger with longer faces and flowing tails. Donkeys often have longer ears and stockier bodies.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Donkeys usually have longer ears.
Horses are often taller.
Horses often have fuller flowing tails.
Donkeys often have a stockier build.

teachingPoint: Goats often have straighter hair, beards, and upward tails. Sheep often have woolly coats and downward tails.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Goats often have beards.
Sheep often have woolly coats.
Goat tails often point up.
Sheep tails often point down.

teachingPoint: Ducks are usually smaller with shorter necks. Geese are usually larger with longer necks.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Ducks are usually smaller.
Geese usually have longer necks.
Geese are often larger and taller.
Ducks often have broader, flatter bills.

teachingPoint: Ravens are usually larger with thicker beaks and wedge-shaped tails. Crows are usually smaller with fan-shaped tails.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Ravens are usually larger than crows.
Ravens often have thicker, heavier beaks.
Ravens may have wedge-shaped tails.
Crows often have fan-shaped tails.

teachingPoint: Owls often have round faces and forward-facing eyes. Hawks usually have sleeker heads and hooked beaks for daytime hunting.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Owls often have round facial disks.
Hawks usually have sleeker heads.
Owls have very forward-facing eyes.
Hawks are often seen soaring in the daytime.

teachingPoint: Crabs usually have wide flat bodies and walk sideways. Lobsters have longer bodies with large claws and a tail.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Crabs have wide, flat bodies.
Lobsters have longer bodies.
Crabs often walk sideways.
Lobsters have a long tail section.

teachingPoint: Shrimp usually have slender curved bodies and no large front claws. Crawfish look like tiny lobsters with claws.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Shrimp are usually slender and curved.
Crawfish have small lobster-like claws.
Crawfish look like tiny lobsters.
Shrimp usually do not have large front claws.

teachingPoint: Zebras have black-and-white stripes. Horses usually have solid or patchy coats without zebra stripes.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Zebras have black-and-white stripes.
Horses usually do not have zebra stripes.
Zebras are wild equines.
Horses are often domesticated.

teachingPoint: Moose are much larger with broad flat antlers and long faces. Deer are usually smaller with slimmer bodies and branching antlers.
distinguishingCharacteristics: Moose are much larger than most deer.
Moose often have broad, flat antlers.
Deer usually have slimmer bodies.
Deer antlers are often branching rather than broad and flat.
